sentence: Part~VI treats Core Theory as a production compiler surface---not a tutorial.
title: PyTorch 2.0: The Journey to Bringing Compiler Technologies to the Core of PyTorch (Keynote)

sentence: The mistake to avoid is optimizing prefill FLOPs while batch-1 decode misses p99 SLOs .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

sentence: Memory-Floor CUDA Graphs on Qwen-2.5-7B (BS , ctx ) yields on H100 by removing host overhead---yet cannot fix illegal HBM traffic .
title: CUDA Graphs

sentence: figure[t] tikzpicture[vis base, scale=0.88, transform shape] (s0) at (-5.03,0) Input\ Theory; (s1) at (-1.68,0) On-chip\ / residency; (s2) at (1.67,0) Fused compute\ stage; (s3) at (5.03,0) Output\ layer / KV; (s0.east) -- (s1.west); (s1.east) -- (s2.west); (s2.east) -- (s3.west); tikzpicture Tiling Theory optimization pipeline in Core Theory: AI Compiler Optimization Principles.
title: Flow-Graph-Aware Tiling and Rescheduling for Memory-Efficient On-Device Inference
sentence_keywords: core theory: ai compiler optimization principles

sentence: example Decode cell (BS , ctx ): unfused tiling raises bytes/token; fused schedule must keep tiling on-chip across GPU, CPU, and NPU baselines .
title: CPU-GPU Workload Distribution during Throughput-Oriented LLM Inference on Single-GPU Systems

sentence: The usual Core Theory failure at tiling theory is importing a CUDA mental model and expecting runtime scheduling to hide illegal buffer lifetimes.
title: Flow-Graph-Aware Tiling and Rescheduling for Memory-Efficient On-Device Inference

sentence: On GPUs that means shared-memory/TMA budgets and warp-grain barriers; on XDNA/AIE it means static tile buffers, DMA chains, and carrier slots with compile-time lifetimes; on CPUs it means L2/L3 blocking and NUMA-local KV placement .
title: CPU-GPU Workload Distribution during Throughput-Oriented LLM Inference on Single-GPU Systems

sentence: Decode at batch-1 keeps arithmetic intensity on the memory-bandwidth slope---micro-optimizing isolated GEMMs rarely moves p99 latency.
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

sentence: Compiler takeaway for tiling theory: lower the same decoder IR, but predicate passes on target residency tables---never ship a GPU-only schedule to NPU fleets .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

sentence: Success metrics: bytes/token, launches/token, and p99 decode latency; compare GPU, CPU, and NPU cells on identical context lengths.
title: CPU-GPU Workload Distribution during Throughput-Oriented LLM Inference on Single-GPU Systems

sentence: A Hopper decode cell may legalize tiling with TMA-backed double buffering while an XDNA cell requires the same math expressed as static tile assignments---identical FLOPs, different bytes/token because DDR traffic differs by an order of magnitude .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

sentence: Teams that A/B only prefill confuse the story: tiling constraints bite hardest at batch-1 decode where launches/token and KV read bandwidth dominate .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

sentence: On GPUs that means shared-memory/TMA budgets and warp-grain barriers; on XDNA/AIE it means static tile buffers, DMA chains, and carrier slots with compile-time lifetimes; on CPUs it means L2/L3 blocking and NUMA-local KV placement .
